title: Disorders in Head Shape and Size
authors: Zhou X, Tomei K / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Disorders in Head Shape and Size?
ทุก morning round เริ่มต้นด้วยการวัด OFC — แต่ neonatologist ที่ดีต้องรู้มากกว่าตัวเลข. (1) Head circumference คือ surrogate ของ intracranial volume — OFC ที่โตเร็วเกินไปหรือหยุดโตคือ red flag ที่ต้องเอา US มาตรวจทันที; (2) Craniosynostosis incidence ~1:2,000 live births แต่ถ้า miss → elevated ICP → brain damage ที่ป้องกันได้; (3) Back to Sleep campaign เพิ่ม referral สำหรับ plagiocephaly 400% ใน 2004–2007 — ต้องแยก positional plagiocephaly จาก lambdoid synostosis ได้ก่อน refer; (4) Post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus ในทารก VLBW ต้องการการ monitor OFC ทุกวัน + threshold สำหรับ tap/shunt ที่ชัดเจน; (5) Syndromic craniosynostosis (Apert, Crouzon, Muenke) มี airway + ophthalmologic + developmental issues ที่ต้องวางแผนร่วมกัน multidisciplinary ตั้งแต่ NICU. ศาสตร์ของ head shape คือ window เข้าสู่ brain development.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
อธิบาย technique ที่ถูกต้องของการวัด OFC, fontanelle assessment, และ normal head growth rate ใน 3 ช่วงแรกของชีวิต
กำหนด microcephaly และ macrocephaly (standard deviation-based definition) และ list ตาม etiology
อธิบาย CSF physiology: rate of production (0.3 mL/min), total CSF volume in newborn (50 mL), choroid plexus → arachnoid villi pathway
จำแนก hydrocephalus เป็น communicating vs non-communicating และ congenital vs acquired; ระบุสาเหตุแต่ละประเภท
อธิบาย IVH grading (Papile grade 1–4) และ mechanism ของ post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus (arachnoiditis → malfunctioning arachnoid villi)
วินิจฉัย Dandy-Walker malformation (classic triad: cystic 4th ventricle, cerebellar vermis agenesis, enlarged posterior fossa) และแยกจาก Chiari II malformation
อธิบาย hydranencephaly: etiology (bilateral carotid occlusion, 2nd trimester), clinical presentation, prognosis, ทำไมถึง appear normal at birth
อธิบาย pathomechanism ของ craniosynostosis (FGFR mutations, dura signals), ระบุ head shapes + fused sutures (Table 59.1)
แยก positional plagiocephaly จาก lambdoid craniosynostosis โดยใช้ ear position และ head shape จาก top view
ระบุ nonsyndromic craniosynostosis types: sagittal (scaphocephaly), metopic (trigonocephaly), unilateral coronal (harlequin eye), bicoronal (brachycephaly)
จำ syndromic craniosynostosis (Muenke FGFR3, Apert FGFR2, Crouzon FGFR2, Pfeiffer, Saethre-Chotzen) พร้อม distinguishing features
วางแผน NICU management: OFC monitoring, ventricular tap thresholds, referral timing สำหรับ neurosurgery
1. Examination of the Head
1.1 OFC Measurement — Technique
Occipitofrontal circumference (OFC): tape measure firmly pressed against scalp; include occiput at/above external occipital protuberance (inion) AND forehead just above glabella
Age-adjusted measurements essential for preterm infants — use Fenton chart หรือ corrected age-based growth charts
Definition: macrocephaly = OFC > +2 SD; microcephaly = OFC < −2 SD; severe microcephaly = OFC < −3 SD
1.2 Fontanelle Assessment
Anterior fontanelle: junction ของ frontal + parietal bones; intersection ของ metopic, coronal, sagittal sutures; normally soft, flat, pulsatile with heartbeat
Tense/full → normal when crying; sustained bulging → elevated ICP (pathologic)
Closure timing: anterior fontanelle: usually by 18 months (range up to 24 months; mean 16.3 months boys, 18.8 months girls — Pindrik et al Clin Pediatr 2014)
Posterior fontanelle: junction ของ parietal + occipital bones at sagittal + lambdoid sutures; closes by 3 months
Palpate sutures for abnormal splaying (suture separation = ↑ ICP) หรือ overlap (premature fusion = craniosynostosis)
1.3 Normal Head Growth Rate
OFC ที่ decelerate หรือ accelerate ออกนอก curve → investigate immediately (US cranial ±MRI)
2. Microcephaly และ Macrocephaly — Etiology
2.1 Microcephaly
Primary microcephaly: brain never grows normally → genetic/embryologic cause; present at birth
Secondary microcephaly: brain reached expected size then fails to grow → acquired/postnatal cause

Embryologic causes: defects in neurulation (→ neural tube defects + absent cranial vault in anencephaly); abnormal cleavage (holoprosencephaly); migration disorders (lissencephaly, schizencephaly, polymicrogyria)
TORCH infections: toxoplasmosis, rubella, CMV, HSV, Zika — all cause microcephaly; CMV most common congenital viral infection; Zika: severe microcephaly with cortical destruction in 2016 epidemic
Other acquired: HI, severe malnutrition, FAS, maternal PKU, ionizing radiation, anticonvulsants (valproate)

Macrencephaly vs Hydrocephalus vs Neoplasm — Three Causes of Macrocephaly
1. Macrencephaly: enlarged brain itself; seen in neurocutaneous syndromes (neurofibromatosis, tuberous sclerosis, Sotos syndrome), metabolic disorders (Alexander disease, Canavan disease), familial megalencephaly. 2. Hydrocephalus: enlargement of CSF spaces — most common cause of macrocephaly in neonates; communicating vs non-communicating; congenital vs acquired. 3. Neoplasms / vascular lesions: choroid plexus papilloma → CSF overproduction; vein of Galen malformation → high-output failure + macrocephaly; rarely present at birth unless formed in late 3rd trimester.
3. Hydrocephalus — Pathophysiology and Classification
3.1 CSF Physiology
CSF production: ~0.3 mL/min = 20 mL/h = 432–500 mL/day (predominantly choroid plexus); lower rate in premature infants
Total CSF volume in newborn: ~50 mL; adult ~150 mL

Pathway: choroid plexus → lateral ventricles → foramina of Monro → 3rd ventricle → aqueduct of Sylvius → 4th ventricle → foramina of Luschka + Magendie → subarachnoid space → arachnoid villi → venous sinuses
Impaired balance (overproduction หรือ underabsorption) → enlargement ของ CSF spaces = hydrocephalus
3.2 Clinical Presentation by Suture Status
Open sutures (newborn/infant): macrocephaly + tense/bulging fontanelle + split sutures — classic neonatal presentation
Closed sutures (>2 years): no macrocephaly but symptoms of elevated ICP: headache, irritability, vomiting, lethargy, 'sunset' eyes (Parinaud sign = forced downgaze from tectal compression)
3.3 Classification

rows: Aqueductal stenosis, Dandy-Walker, Chiari II, neoplasm, vein of Galen malformation
Aqueductal stenosis (10%), Dandy-Walker, Chiari II, holoprosencephaly
Post-IVH (most common in preterm), post-meningitis, neoplasm

blocks.text: 3.4 Aqueductal Stenosis
Accounts for ~10% of pediatric hydrocephalus; congenital or acquired (neoplasm compressing aqueduct of Sylvius)
Radiographic pattern: lateral + 3rd ventricle enlargement with NORMAL 4th ventricle — key distinguishing feature
Macrocephaly not always present at birth; may progress before suture closure if stenosis is severe
3.5 Vein of Galen Malformation
Rare vascular cause of macrocephaly + hydrocephalus; arises 3rd trimester from persistent embryonic prosencephalic vein of Markowski
Presentation: high-output heart failure + macrocephaly + hydrocephalus — classic triad; often diagnosed prenatally by fetal US
Treatment: endovascular embolization = mainstay; hydrocephalus may require VP shunt or third ventriculostomy in children who cannot undergo endovascular treatment or need staged repair
4. Intraventricular Hemorrhage และ Post-Hemorrhagic Hydrocephalus
4.1 IVH Pathophysiology
Germinal matrix: rich vascular network in periventricular subependyma; normally involutes by 36 weeks' gestation; in preterm, involution incomplete → fragile vessels

Incidence: up to 27% ของ preterm infants <1500 g (Szpecht et al Childs Nerv Syst 2016); term infants can also develop IVH — usually associated with coagulopathy (Afsharkhas et al Iran J Child Neurol 2015)
4.2 Papile IVH Grading

rows: Blood confined to germinal matrix only

blocks.text: Close observation recommended for IVH grades 3–4: monitor OFC, ventricular size on serial cranial US
4.3 Post-Hemorrhagic Hydrocephalus (PHVD)
Definition: ventriculomegaly + signs of elevated ICP including ↑ OFC
Mechanism: blood in subarachnoid space → arachnoiditis → malfunctioning arachnoid villi → impaired CSF reabsorption (communicating hydrocephalus)

Management Ladder สำหรับ Post-Hemorrhagic Hydrocephalus
Step 1 — Limit CSF production: acetazolamide (Diamox) → carbonic anhydrase inhibitor → ↓ CSF production; limited evidence for outcome benefit but used as temporizing measure. Step 2 — CSF removal: serial lumbar punctures (if communicating), serial ventricular taps (via fontanelle), Ommaya reservoir placement (Peretta Childs Nerv Syst 2007), or ventriculosubgaleal shunt (Petraglia et al Pediatr Neurosurg 2011). Step 3 — Permanent diversion: ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt when infant is clinically stable and large enough; timing varies by institution. Note: repeated ventricular reservoir taps do NOT increase risk of reservoir infection (Kormanik et al J Perinatol 2010).
5. Congenital Brain Malformations ที่ทำให้ Macrocephaly
5.1 Dandy-Walker Malformation

Classic triad: (1) cystic transformation ของ 4th ventricle; (2) partial หรือ complete agenesis ของ cerebellar vermis; (3) enlarged posterior cranial fossa + elevated torcula herophili + elevated tentorium
Incidence: ~1:25,000 live births; more common in females; usually sporadic
Associated malformations: occipital encephalocele/meningocele, cleft lip/palate, other craniofacial malformations frequently observed
Hydrocephalus: supratentorial ventriculomegaly present at birth or progresses over time; obstructive
External sign: high-riding external occipital protuberance on physical exam
Prognosis: determined largely by associated malformations; other brain malformations in 70% of cases (Spennato et al Childs Nerv Syst 2011); isolated DWM has variable prognosis
5.2 Chiari II Malformation
Characteristics: abnormal hindbrain secondary to small posterior fossa; low-lying transverse sinuses + tentorium; beaked tectum; herniation of cerebellar vermis below foramen magnum
Uniformly associated with: lumbosacral myelomeningocele — this is the pathologic link; not seen without MMC
OFC at birth: may be NORMAL or even SMALL at birth (decompression of CSF into myelomeningocele sac); rapid OFC expansion occurs AFTER MMC closure → monitor closely post-op
Untreated: obstructive hydrocephalus → cranial neuropathies + brainstem dysfunction from downward pressure
Management: MMC closure within 24h → monitor OFC post-op → VP shunt when hydrocephalus develops
6. Hydranencephaly
Definition: rare congenital anomaly — almost complete absence of cerebral hemispheres

Etiology: major in utero vascular insult during 2nd trimester, typically bilateral occlusion of internal carotid arteries → brain undergoes intrauterine liquefaction → meningeal sac containing CSF with high protein
What is spared: diencephalon (thalami), brainstem, posterior fossa structures; thin occipital cortical rim may permit some visual function
Clinical presentation at birth: may appear neurologically normal — normally functioning thalami + brainstem; difficulty with visual fixing/following may be appreciated
Head size: normal or small at birth → can enlarge rapidly due to malabsorption of proteinaceous CSF
Diagnosis may be delayed months due to failure to achieve developmental milestones
Diagnosis: cranial US (limited) vs MRI much more accurate — show absence of hemispheres with intact meninges + skull (distinguishes from anencephaly)

Hydranencephaly vs Advanced Hydrocephalus — Critical Distinction
Hydranencephaly: absent cerebral hemispheres; intact meninges + skull; CSF with HIGH protein; no recoverable cortex. Advanced hydrocephalus: compressed cerebral mantle present; CSF protein normal/slightly elevated; mantle CAN expand dramatically after VP shunt. Clinical implication: shunting hydranencephaly is done ONLY to make care easier (reduce head size), NOT to improve neurologic function — must counsel family clearly. Prognosis hydranencephaly: poor; most do not live more than 1–2 years. Always distinguish before offering surgical intervention.
7. Craniosynostosis — Overview และ Pathomechanism
7.1 Epidemiology และ Pathomechanism
Definition: premature fusion ของ cranial sutures — hallmark is restriction of perpendicular growth to fused suture + compensatory growth at non-fused sites
Incidence: ~1:2,000 live births; single suture = majority; multiple suture = syndromic forms

Elevated ICP: estimated 4.5–44% ของ untreated children (Wall et al J Neurosurg Pediatr 2014); majority improve ICP symptoms after surgery but fewer have resolution of developmental delay (Still et al J Craniofac Surg 2021)

FGFR mutations: fibroblast growth factor receptors (FGFR1, 2, 3) most commonly implicated (Gripp et al J Pediatr 1998); dura normally promotes patent sutures until completion of brain growth — signals altered in craniosynostosis
Secondary causes: metabolic/hematologic — rickets, hyperthyroidism, polycythemia vera, thalassemia → secondary suture fusion
7.2 Head Shape Terminology — Table 59.1
7.3 Diagnosis
Physical exam: first step — knowledge of head shapes → identifies fused suture; in neonates, sutures relatively mobile → absent mobility along suture of concern = positive finding
Cranial ultrasound: noninvasive, no radiation; sensitivity 71–100%, specificity 86–100% (Whitsell & Meyer S D Med 2021) — useful in young infants with thin bones
Plain skull X-ray: sclerosis along all or part of fused suture — confirmatory
CT scan: highest sensitivity; useful in older infants where suture mobility less palpable; essential for operative planning (Whitsell 2021)
8. Nonsyndromic Craniosynostosis
8.1 Sagittal Craniosynostosis
Most common type: premature sagittal suture fusion; incidence ~1:1,000 live births; 4:1 male:female preponderance (Governale LS Pediatr Neurol 2015)
Head shape — scaphocephaly: narrowed biparietal diameter + compensatory frontal bossing + temporal hollowing + prominent occipital protuberance = 'boat-shaped head'
Neurologically: most often normal
Surgery: minimally invasive (endoscopic) for younger infants + post-op helmet therapy; open cranial vault remodeling for older patients
8.2 Metopic Craniosynostosis
Head shape — trigonocephaly: triangular-shaped forehead with vertical keel; reduced anterior cranial vault + hypotelorism + elevation of lateral canthi + temporal hollowing
Surgical correction: endoscopic procedure หรือ bifrontal craniotomy with orbital bar advancement
8.3 Coronal Craniosynostosis

Unilateral vs Bilateral Coronal Craniosynostosis — Clinical Differences
Unilateral coronal craniosynostosis: oblique head shape; ipsilateral frontal flattening + compensatory contralateral bossing + ipsilateral nasal root deviation. 'Harlequin eye' deformity on X-ray: ipsilateral orbit drawn upward and backward (superolateral margin elevated) — pathognomonic on plain skull film or 3D CT. Bilateral coronal (brachycephaly): palpable ridges over BOTH coronal sutures; head foreshortened in A-P plane; may have compensatory ↑ height = turricephaly. Important: ALL children with bicoronal synostosis should be evaluated to rule out underlying syndrome — bicoronal fusion is a hallmark of syndromic craniosynostosis (Governale 2015).
8.4 Positional Plagiocephaly vs Lambdoid Synostosis

Positional (deformational) plagiocephaly: far more common cause of asymmetric head shape; incidence ↑↑ after 'Back to Sleep' campaign (Branch LG et al J Craniofac Surg 2015: 400% ↑ in plastic surgery referrals 2004–2007)
Lambdoid synostosis: rare — ~3% ของ all craniosynostosis cases

rows: Ipsilateral ear displaced POSTERIORLY and INFERIORLY

blocks.text: Mnemonic: 'Position = ear goes Forward (same direction as the force)'; Lambdoid = ear is Pulled Back (by the fixed suture)
9. Syndromic Craniosynostosis
9.1 Overview
Most commonly inherited in autosomal dominant fashion; most frequent syndromes involve FGFR mutations
Common features to all: sensorineural hearing loss, respiratory difficulties (midface hypoplasia), cognitive deficits ± hydrocephalus
9.2 Individual Syndromes

rows: AD; variable: unilateral to pansynostosis; coronal often bilateral; sensorineural hearing loss most common; mild midface hypoplasia + hypertelorism
Mostly sporadic (± AD); brachyturricephaly + flattened occiput; maxillary hypoplasia; widely open anterior fontanelle; SYMMETRIC hand + foot SYNDACTYLY; cognitive delay
Coronal sutures typical → brachycephaly; anomalies NOT present at birth (appear later); exophthalmos + maxillary hypoplasia; NO syndactyly; cognitive delay infrequent
Coronal + sagittal + lambdoid involvement; maxillary hypoplasia + choanal atresia; BROAD THUMBS + broad great toes — hallmark extracranial feature
Brachycephaly; low-lying hairline; ptosis; hypertelorism; mild facial asymmetry

blocks.text: Apert vs Crouzon: both FGFR2, both have brachycephaly + exophthalmos + maxillary hypoplasia; Apert = + syndactyly + cognitive delay; Crouzon = anomalies appear later (not at birth) + no syndactyly + cognitive usually normal
10. NICU Practical — Head Shape and Size Assessment
10.1 OFC Monitoring Protocol
Measure OFC daily: ทุก VLBW; every visit ทุก NICU patient ที่มี IVH หรือ hydrocephalus risk; plot on age-corrected Fenton chart
Alarm thresholds: OFC crossing upward across 2 major percentile lines หรือ >1 cm/day → urgent cranial US
Document fontanelle: size + tension + pulsatility with each nursing shift for at-risk infants
10.2 PHVD Management — When to Tap
Indications for ventricular tap: rapidly ↑ OFC + tense fontanelle + clinical signs of ↑ ICP (apnea, bradycardia, bulging fontanelle, split sutures)
Reservoir vs serial taps: Ommaya reservoir allows repeated tapping without fresh needle trauma; no increased infection risk with repeated tapping (Kormanik 2010)
Serial lumbar punctures: only if communicating hydrocephalus (blood flows to spinal canal)
VP shunt timing: typically deferred until infant >1.5–2 kg and CSF protein <100–200 mg/dL to minimize shunt blockage; coordinate with neurosurgery
10.3 Craniosynostosis — When to Refer
Early referral to craniofacial surgery + neurosurgery: any infant with abnormal head shape + palpable suture ridge or absent suture mobility
Ophthalmology referral: all syndromic cases (exophthalmos risk = corneal exposure; papilledema = elevated ICP)
Audiology: all syndromic cases (Muenke especially — SNHL most common feature)
Surgical timing: minimally invasive: <6 months preferred; open vault remodeling: 6–12 months; early intervention allows brain growth to guide head remodeling
10.4 Positional Plagiocephaly — Prevention and Management
Prevention: tummy time when awake + supervised; alternate head positions during sleep; avoid excessive time in bouncy seats/car seats
Management: repositioning exercises; physiotherapy (if torticollis present → most common contributing factor); cranial helmet orthosis if not resolving by 4–5 months
Natural history: most cases improve with conservative management alone; helmet therapy most effective when started before 8 months of age
11. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — OFC Crossing Upward = Act Now, Not Tomorrow
OFC ที่ขยับข้าม 2 percentile lines ขึ้น หรือโตเกิน 1 cm/day ใน NICU infant คือ neurosurgical emergency จนกว่าจะพิสูจน์ว่าไม่ใช่. ทำ cranial US เองทันที แทนที่จะรอ consultant. สิ่งที่ต้องหา: (1) ventricular enlargement (PHVD); (2) germinal matrix bleed ใหม่; (3) subdural collection; (4) dandy-walker ที่ hydrocephalus เพิ่งดำเนินต่อ. การ delay 24–48h = risk ของ brain herniation. Tense bulging fontanelle = ↑ ICP จนกว่าจะพิสูจน์ได้ว่าไม่ใช่.

PEARL 2 — Ear Position แยก Positional Plagiocephaly จาก Lambdoid Synostosis ได้ทันที
ดูจาก top view: positional plagiocephaly = parallelogram shape; ipsilateral ear FORWARD (ear shift ตาม direction ที่กดแบน = หัวดัน ear ไปข้างหน้า). Lambdoid synostosis = suture fusion ดึง ear ไป POSTERIOR and INFERIOR (growth restricted by fused suture → ear pulled back). ถ้า ear ไปข้างหน้า = safe to observe with repositioning. ถ้า ear ไปข้างหลัง = refer neurosurgery ทันที (true synostosis; 3% of all craniosynostosis). Physical exam ใช้เวลา 30 วินาที แต่ช่วยหลีกเลี่ยง unnecessary imaging ใน positional cases.

PEARL 3 — Apert ≠ Crouzon: Syndactyly + Timing เป็น Key
ทั้ง Apert และ Crouzon มี FGFR2 mutations, coronal synostosis, brachycephaly, exophthalmos, maxillary hypoplasia. ความแตกต่างที่สำคัญ: (1) Apert = symmetric hand + foot syndactyly (ค้นหา webbing เสมอ ไม่ใช่แค่หัว); (2) Apert = cognitive delay เป็น rule; (3) Crouzon = anomalies NOT present at birth (diagnosis often later in infancy); (4) Crouzon = cognitive delay infrequent. Bicoronal synostosis ใน newborn ควร evaluate สำหรับ syndrome เสมอ — ถ้าไม่ syndromic ก็ rare มาก.

PEARL 4 — Hydranencephaly: May Look Normal at Birth — Don't Be Fooled
ทารกที่มี hydranencephaly อาจ appear neurologically normal ใน 24–48h แรก เพราะ thalami + brainstem ยังทำงานได้ปกติ. Signs ที่ออกมาทีหลัง: failure ของ visual fixation/following, developmental plateau, หัวโตขึ้นจาก CSF accumulation. Cranial US อาจมองเห็น CSF-filled sac แต่ MRI เท่านั้นที่ distinguish ได้ชัดเจนจาก severe hydrocephalus. สำคัญที่สุด: VP shunt ใน hydranencephaly ทำได้ แต่เป้าหมายคือ ease of care เท่านั้น ไม่ใช่การ restore neurologic function — ต้องคุยกับครอบครัวก่อนทำ shunt อย่างตรงไปตรงมา.

PEARL 5 — Chiari II OFC ที่ 'เล็กผิดปกติ' ตอนแรก ≠ ไม่มีปัญหา
ทารกที่มี myelomeningocele + Chiari II malformation อาจมี OFC ที่ normal หรือแม้แต่เล็กกว่า expected ตอน birth เพราะ CSF decompresses เข้าไปใน myelomeningocele sac แทนที่จะสะสมในสมอง. หลังจาก MMC ถูก surgically closed ภายใน 24h → CSF กลับไปสะสมในสมอง → OFC อาจ expand อย่างรวดเร็ว → monitor OFC ทุกวันหลัง closure + threshold ต่ำสำหรับ VP shunt. อย่า false reassure ว่า 'OFC เล็กในเด็ก MMC = ไม่มีปัญหา' เพราะนี่คือ false negative ที่อันตราย.
12. Evidence Update — Key Studies

Pindrik J et al Clin Pediatr (Phila) 2014;53:1149: anterior fontanelle closure in full-term children based on head CT; mean 16.3 months (boys) / 18.8 months (girls); reference for normal anterior fontanelle closure timing
Szpecht D et al Childs Nerv Syst 2016;32:1399: IVH in neonates <32 weeks: up to 27% of preterm infants <1500 g affected; retrospective analysis of risk factors
Afsharkhas L et al Iran J Child Neurol 2015;9:34: IVH in term neonates: sources, severity, outcome — term IVH usually associated with underlying coagulopathy
Kormanik K et al J Perinatol 2010;30:218: repeated tapping of ventricular reservoir in preterm PHVD does NOT increase risk of infection — supports serial tapping strategy
Peretta P et al Childs Nerv Syst 2007;23:765: Ommaya reservoir + endoscopic third ventriculostomy in post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus of prematurity — management options review
Petraglia AL et al Pediatr Neurosurg 2011;47:99: ventriculosubgaleal shunting — strategy to reduce shunt revisions and slit ventricles in PHVD
Spennato P et al Childs Nerv Syst 2011;27:1665: hydrocephalus in Dandy-Walker malformation: other brain malformations in 70% of cases; isolated DWM variable prognosis
McLone DG, Knepper PA Pediatr Neurosci 1989;15:1: unified theory of Chiari II malformation — pathogenesis linked to myelomeningocele CSF decompression
Branch LG et al J Craniofac Surg 2015;26:147: deformational plagiocephaly and craniosynostosis trends after 'Back to Sleep' campaign: 400% increase in plastic surgery referrals 2004–2007
Wall SA et al J Neurosurg Pediatr 2014;14:674: elevated ICP in nonsyndromic sagittal craniosynostosis underestimated in literature; preoperative ICP assessment important
Still ME et al J Craniofac Surg 2021: craniosynostosis surgery for elevated ICP: majority improved ICP symptoms (headache) but fewer improved developmental delay
Governale LS Pediatr Neurol 2015;53:394: comprehensive craniosynostosis review: sagittal most common (1:1000, 4:1 M:F); FGFR pathway; management principles
Gripp KW et al J Pediatr 1998;132:714: identification of FGFR3 mutation as genetic cause for isolated unilateral coronal synostosis
Wang JC et al Facial Plast Surg Clin North Am 2016;24:531: syndromic craniosynostosis review: Muenke, Apert, Crouzon, Pfeiffer, Saethre-Chotzen — distinguishing features and genetics
Whitsell N, Meyer L S D Med 2021;74:83: craniosynostosis review for providers: diagnosis criteria, imaging (US sensitivity 71–100%, specificity 86–100%), management ladder
Dempsey RF et al Clin Plast Surg 2019;46:123: nonsyndromic craniosynostosis: minimally invasive vs open techniques; early intervention for brain growth guidance; helmet therapy post-endoscopic
Primikiris P et al Expert Rev Med Devices 2021;18:933: vein of Galen malformations: current treatment status — endovascular as mainstay; shunt/third ventriculostomy for refractory hydrocephalus
13. Self-Check Q&A
Q: วิธีวัด OFC ที่ถูกต้อง และ head growth ปกติในแต่ละช่วงอายุเป็นอย่างไร?

ตอบ: Technique: tape measure firmly pressed against scalp; occiput ที่/เหนือ inion (external occipital protuberance) AND forehead เหนือ glabella. Normal growth: first 3 months = ~2 cm/month; months 4–6 = ~1 cm/month; months 7–12 = ~0.5 cm/month. OFC crossing upward 2 major percentile lines หรือ >1 cm/day → cranial US urgently. Preterm: use corrected age-adjusted growth charts (Fenton chart); plot consistently at each weight measurement.
Q: อธิบาย CSF physiology และ mechanism ที่ทำให้เกิด PHVD ใน IVH preterm

ตอบ: CSF production: 0.3 mL/min (~20 mL/h); total newborn CSF ~50 mL; choroid plexus → lateral ventricles → foramina of Monro → 3rd ventricle → aqueduct → 4th ventricle → subarachnoid → arachnoid villi → venous sinuses. PHVD mechanism: IVH blood → subarachnoid space → arachnoiditis → malfunctioning arachnoid villi → impaired CSF reabsorption → ventriculomegaly + elevated ICP. Definition: ventriculomegaly + signs of ↑ ICP including ↑ OFC.
Q: Dandy-Walker malformation: triad คืออะไร และต้องแยกจากอะไร?

ตอบ: Classic triad: (1) cystic transformation ของ 4th ventricle; (2) partial/complete cerebellar vermis agenesis; (3) enlarged posterior fossa + elevated torcula herophili + elevated tentorium. External sign: high-riding external occipital protuberance. Differential: enlarged cisterna magna (large posterior fossa CSF but no cyst + intact vermis), arachnoid cyst. 70% ของ DWM มี associated brain malformations → prognosis determined by these, not by DWM itself.
Q: Chiari II malformation: ทำไม OFC ถึง small/normal ตอน birth และต้องทำอะไรหลัง MMC closure?

ตอบ: Small OFC mechanism: CSF decompresses into myelomeningocele sac แทนที่จะสะสมใน ventricles → OFC normal หรือ small ตอน birth. After MMC closure (within 24h): CSF drainage pathway removed → CSF accumulates in brain → OFC อาจ expand rapidly. Monitor: OFC daily post-MMC closure; low threshold for VP shunt; obstructive hydrocephalus may manifest as cranial neuropathies (downward pressure on brainstem) if untreated.
Q: แยก positional plagiocephaly จาก lambdoid synostosis โดยใช้ ear position

ตอบ: Positional plagiocephaly (parallelogram shape): ipsilateral ear displaced FORWARD; both ears in same horizontal plane; ipsilateral occipital flattening + ipsilateral frontal prominence. Lambdoid synostosis (trapezoid shape): ipsilateral ear displaced POSTERIORLY + INFERIORLY (growth restricted perpendicular to fused suture); contralateral frontal bossing. Management: positional → repositioning + physio ± helmet; lambdoid (3% of craniosynostosis) → surgical referral.
Q: IVH grading: Papile grade 1–4 คืออะไร และ grade ไหนที่ต้องเฝ้าระวัง PHVD?

ตอบ: Grade 1: blood confined to germinal matrix only. Grade 2: blood within ventricles WITHOUT enlargement. Grade 3: blood within ventricles WITH ventriculomegaly. Grade 4: hemorrhage extending into brain parenchyma (periventricular hemorrhagic infarction). PHVD risk: primarily grades 3–4 → monitor OFC daily + serial cranial US + watch for clinical signs of elevated ICP (tense fontanelle, apnea/bradycardia, split sutures). Higher grade = worse prognosis; grade 4 = worst (parenchymal destruction).
Q: Hydranencephaly: mechanism, presentation ที่ tricky, prognosis, management decision

ตอบ: Mechanism: bilateral carotid occlusion in 2nd trimester → brain liquefaction → meningeal sac of high-protein CSF. Diencephalon, brainstem, posterior fossa spared. Tricky presentation: may appear neurologically normal at birth (thalami + brainstem intact); visual deficits + developmental failure appear later. Prognosis: poor; most die within 1–2 years. VP shunt decision: shunting done ONLY to reduce head size for ease of care — does NOT improve neurologic function; must counsel family explicitly.
Q: Apert syndrome vs Crouzon syndrome: เหมือนกันตรงไหน ต่างกันตรงไหน?

ตอบ: Same: both FGFR2 mutations; both coronal synostosis → brachycephaly; exophthalmos; maxillary hypoplasia. Apert features: (1) symmetric hand + foot syndactyly — hallmark; (2) widely open anterior fontanelle; (3) cognitive delay (rule); mostly sporadic. Crouzon features: (1) craniofacial anomalies NOT present at birth — develop during infancy; (2) NO syndactyly; (3) cognitive delay infrequent. Identify Apert by hands/feet + onset at birth; Crouzon by late presentation + normal hands.
Q: Sagittal craniosynostosis: incidence, head shape, surgical approach

ตอบ: Most common craniosynostosis type: incidence ~1:1,000; 4:1 M:F. Scaphocephaly: narrowed biparietal diameter + frontal bossing + temporal hollowing + prominent occipital protuberance ('boat head'). Neurologically normal in most. Surgery: minimally invasive (endoscopic) best <6 months + requires post-op helmet; open vault remodeling for older patients; early intervention → brain growth guides remodeling naturally.
Q: Muenke syndrome: gene, frequency, distinctive feature ที่ต่างจาก syndromic craniosynostosis อื่น

ตอบ: Most common syndromic craniosynostosis: ~1:30,000; FGFR3 gene; autosomal dominant. Coronal suture usually involved (bilateral more common than unilateral); highly variable presentation from single suture to pansynostosis. Distinctive feature: sensorineural hearing loss MORE common than other syndromes → audiologic evaluation essential. Mild midface hypoplasia + hypertelorism. All children with Muenke need audiology follow-up + hearing aids if SNHL confirmed.

Day 157 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.60
บทต่อไป: Chapter 60 — Spinal Dysraphisms. เตรียมตัว: myelomeningocele (MMC) — anatomy, delivery room protocol, surgical timing; Chiari II malformation management post-MMC closure; tethered spinal cord — occult dysraphisms: intraspinal lipoma, diastematomyelia, dermal sinus tract; bladder management (neurogenic bladder) + UTI prophylaxis + clean intermittent catheterization; long-term multidisciplinary follow-up: neurosurgery + urology + orthopedics + developmental pediatrics; prognostication by lesion level (L2 vs L3 vs L4–5: ambulatory status).
